questions.question: Following Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44, what is the current legal framework governing self-defence in Ireland?

options.A: Self-defence in Ireland continues to be governed by common law principles established in AG v Keatley [1954] — the statutory framework in the NFOATP 1997 applies only to public order incidents
options.B: Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44 held that the common law of self-defence has been abolished by s.18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 — section 18 now governs all cases of self-defence in Irish law, including cases involving fatal force; the common law no longer applies
options.C: Self-defence in Ireland is governed by both the common law and s.18 NFOATP 1997 — the accused can choose which framework to rely upon
options.D: Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44 confirmed that s.18 NFOATP 1997 applies only to non-fatal offences — cases involving fatal force are still governed by common law self-defence

questions.explanation: Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44 is a landmark Irish Supreme Court decision that definitively resolved the question of whether the common law of self-defence survives the enactment of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 (NFOATP 1997). The Supreme Court held that s.18 NFOATP 1997 abolished the common law of self-defence — section 18 is now the sole governing framework for all cases involving force in self-defence, including those involving fatal force (murder/manslaughter cases). This resolved the pre-Crawford uncertainty about whether the common law continued alongside the statute. FE-1 candidates must know: (1) Crawford is the current law; (2) s.18 NFOATP 1997 governs all self-defence cases; (3) the common law no longer applies. Option A incorrectly retains the common law framework. Option C incorrectly allows the accused to choose between frameworks. Option D incorrectly limits s.18 to non-fatal offences.

questions.question: What does s.18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 provide as the test for self-defence?

options.A: Section 18 requires that the force used must have been objectively necessary and proportionate — the accused's belief as to necessity is irrelevant
options.B: Section 18 provides that a person is justified in using force against another where: (1) the person believes the use of force is immediately necessary; and (2) the force used is only such as is reasonable in the circumstances as the person believes them to be — the test is based on the accused's honest belief that force was immediately necessary and reasonable
options.C: Section 18 creates a complete defence only where the force used is the minimum possible to repel the attack — any excess automatically results in conviction
options.D: Section 18 requires that the accused have first attempted to retreat or escape before using force — the duty to retreat remains under the statutory framework

questions.explanation: Section 18 NFOATP 1997 provides the statutory test for self-defence in Irish law following Crawford v DPP [2024]. The test has two components: (1) the accused must have believed that the use of force was immediately necessary — this is a subjective element based on the accused's honest belief, not an objective standard; and (2) the force used must be only such as is reasonable in the circumstances as the accused believed them to be — again, assessed by reference to the circumstances as the accused honestly believed them to be, not necessarily as they objectively were. This creates a primarily subjective test: what did the accused genuinely believe? If the accused's belief in the necessity and reasonableness of the force was honest, even if mistaken, the defence may succeed. Option A incorrectly makes the test purely objective. Option C incorrectly requires minimum possible force. Option D incorrectly retains a duty to retreat — which does not exist under Irish law (see R v McInnes [1971]).

questions.question: Is there a duty to retreat before using force in self-defence under Irish law?

options.A: Yes — Irish law requires that the accused first attempt to retreat or disengage from the situation before using force in self-defence; failure to retreat when possible defeats the defence
options.B: No — there is no duty to retreat under Irish law before using force in self-defence; the principle established in R v McInnes [1971] and adopted in Irish law is that the accused is not required to retreat before defending themselves; willingness to fight does not by itself defeat the defence
options.C: Yes — there is a limited duty to retreat; the accused must retreat if escape is practically possible, but no duty exists where retreat is dangerous
options.D: Yes — but the duty to retreat applies only in public places; there is no duty to retreat from one's own home under the common law

questions.explanation: Irish law does not impose a duty to retreat before using force in self-defence. This principle was confirmed in R v McInnes [1971] (persuasive authority in Ireland) and is consistent with the approach under s.18 NFOATP 1997. The absence of a duty to retreat means that the fact that the accused could have escaped or avoided the confrontation does not by itself defeat the defence of self-defence — the question is whether the force used was necessary in the circumstances as the accused believed them to be, not whether the accused could have avoided the need to use force entirely. However, the availability of an escape route may be relevant evidence going to whether the accused genuinely believed force was necessary. The absence of a duty to retreat should be distinguished from the Castle Doctrine established by the Criminal Law (Defence and Dwelling) Act 2011, which specifically addresses the dwelling context. Options A, C, and D all incorrectly impose some form of duty to retreat under Irish law.

questions.question: In AG v Keatley [1954] (Ireland), the accused used force in self-defence and killed the victim. The court found that the force used was reasonable and proportionate. What was the outcome?

options.A: The court convicted the accused of manslaughter — the use of any fatal force results in at least a manslaughter conviction even if the force was technically reasonable
options.B: The court acquitted the accused — where the accused uses reasonable and proportionate force in self-defence, the result is a complete acquittal; justified self-defence is a full defence that negates criminal liability entirely
options.C: The court convicted of murder but applied a reduced sentence — the conviction for murder stands even where force was reasonable if the outcome was death
options.D: The court found the accused guilty of constructive manslaughter on the basis that death resulted from an unlawful act

questions.explanation: AG v Keatley [1954] is an important Irish authority confirming that successful self-defence (where the force used was reasonable and proportionate) results in a complete acquittal — it is a full defence that negates criminal liability entirely. Where the jury is satisfied that the accused used reasonable and proportionate force in defence of themselves or another, the proper verdict is not guilty. Self-defence, when successfully established, is not a mitigating factor that reduces a murder to manslaughter — it is a complete justification that removes criminal liability. This distinguishes self-defence from the Dwyer doctrine (excess self-defence leading to manslaughter conviction), which applies where the accused honestly believed force was necessary but the force used was excessive. Keatley illustrates the upper end of the self-defence spectrum: proportionate force = complete acquittal. Options A and C incorrectly result in conviction despite reasonable force. Option D incorrectly applies constructive manslaughter.

questions.question: In AG v Dwyer [1972] (Ireland), the accused used excessive force in what they genuinely believed to be self-defence. The court recognised the 'Dwyer doctrine' in Irish law. What does the Dwyer doctrine provide?

options.A: Where an accused uses excessive force in self-defence and kills the victim, the conviction must be for murder — excessive force completely negates the self-defence claim
options.B: Where an accused honestly believed the use of force was necessary in self-defence but used force that was excessive in the circumstances, the result is a conviction for voluntary manslaughter rather than murder — the Dwyer doctrine operates as a partial defence, reducing murder to manslaughter where the accused's belief was honest but the force was objectively excessive
options.C: The Dwyer doctrine provides that any use of force in genuine (if excessive) self-defence results in a complete acquittal — the honesty of the belief is the only relevant consideration
options.D: The Dwyer doctrine has been abolished following Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44 — the enactment of s.18 NFOATP 1997 has eliminated the partial defence of excessive self-defence

questions.explanation: AG v Dwyer [1972] established the Dwyer doctrine in Irish law: where an accused genuinely and honestly believed that the use of force was necessary in self-defence, but the force they used was objectively excessive, the appropriate verdict is voluntary manslaughter rather than murder. This is a partial defence — it does not result in acquittal (unlike complete, proportionate self-defence) but it prevents the conviction being elevated to murder where there was an honest, if excessive, defensive response. The doctrine recognises that a person in a genuine defensive situation who overreacts should not bear the same criminal responsibility as a deliberate murderer. Importantly, the Dwyer doctrine has been confirmed to survive Crawford v DPP [2024] IESC 44 — the Supreme Court confirmed the partial defence of excessive self-defence remains available under s.18 NFOATP 1997. Option A incorrectly results in murder conviction for excessive force. Option C incorrectly acquits for excessive force. Option D incorrectly states Dwyer was abolished by Crawford.

questions.question: In AG v Commane [1975] (Ireland), the accused was attacked, repelled the first attack, but then re-engaged and killed the attacker during a second phase of violence. The court considered whether self-defence was available for the second phase. What principle did this case establish?

options.A: The court held that self-defence was available for the entire incident — once a person is attacked, they are entitled to use whatever force is necessary throughout all phases of the confrontation
options.B: The court held that self-defence must be considered phase by phase — where the first attack has been repelled and the immediate threat has ended, the accused may not claim self-defence for subsequent force used to re-engage or pursue the original attacker; the second phase of violence (after the first threat was repelled) was outside the scope of self-defence and resulted in a murder conviction
options.C: The court held that the continuity of the incident meant that the self-defence claim extended throughout — it was a single incident and the accused's belief in continued threat was sufficient
options.D: The court held that self-defence is available for all force used at the scene of an initial attack, provided the accused genuinely feared future attacks from the same person

questions.explanation: AG v Commane [1975] established the important principle that self-defence is assessed phase by phase — the defence does not extend automatically to all force used throughout an entire altercation. Where the accused successfully repels an initial attack and the immediate threat has ended, any further force used to re-engage, pursue, or attack the original aggressor is not protected by self-defence. In Commane, the first phase of violence (where the accused repelled the initial attack) may have been defensible, but the second phase (where the accused re-engaged after the threat had passed) was found to be outside the scope of self-defence and resulted in a murder conviction. This case is particularly important as it prevents the self-defence doctrine from being used as a licence to settle scores once an initial attack has been repelled. Options A, C, and D all incorrectly extend self-defence beyond the point at which the immediate threat has ended.

questions.question: In DPP v Barnes [2007] (Ireland), the accused broke into a house, was confronted by the householder, and claimed self-defence when the householder used force against him. Can a burglar rely on self-defence against the person whose house they have broken into?

options.A: Yes — self-defence is available to all persons regardless of their prior criminal conduct; a burglar who is attacked has the same right to defend themselves as anyone else
options.B: No — in DPP v Barnes [2007], the Irish courts confirmed that a person who is unlawfully on another's property and has thereby created the confrontational situation cannot rely on self-defence against the lawful occupier who is defending their property and person; the accused's unlawful conduct (the burglary) defeats the self-defence claim
options.C: Yes — self-defence may be available to a burglar but only where the householder uses disproportionate force; a burglar can defend against excessive force
options.D: No — but this rule applies only to burglary; a trespasser in other circumstances retains the right to claim self-defence

questions.explanation: DPP v Barnes [2007] is an important Irish authority on the limits of self-defence for persons engaged in unlawful conduct. The court held that a person who unlawfully enters another's property (a burglar) and is then confronted by the lawful occupier cannot rely on self-defence against that occupier. The rationale is that the burglar created the confrontational situation through their own unlawful conduct — they cannot claim to be acting defensively when they have placed themselves in the situation through criminal trespass. This is consistent with the broader principle (also applicable to duress) that a person who voluntarily creates the circumstances giving rise to a defensive situation cannot rely on that defence. Barnes must be read alongside the Criminal Law (Defence and Dwelling) Act 2011, which strengthens the householder's position by providing a specific defence for use of force against intruders in the dwelling. Options A and C incorrectly extend self-defence to burglars in various circumstances. Option D incorrectly limits the principle to burglary only.

questions.question: The accused provokes another person into attacking them, then claims self-defence when they use force to repel the attack. Is self-defence available where the accused has provoked the confrontation?

options.A: Yes — self-defence is available regardless of how the confrontation began; what matters is the accused's belief at the time of using force
options.B: No — where the accused deliberately provoked another person into attacking them and then used that attack as the basis for a self-defence claim, the defence is unavailable; this is established in cases such as DPP for NI v Browne; the accused cannot manufacture a self-defence situation by provoking another and then claiming to be the victim
options.C: Self-defence is available where the accused provoked the victim, but the verdict is automatically reduced to manslaughter to reflect the accused's contributory fault
options.D: Yes — provocation is a separate doctrine; a person who provokes another is still entitled to use reasonable force in response to the attack they provoked

questions.explanation: The principle established in DPP for NI v Browne and confirmed in Irish case law is that self-defence is unavailable where the accused deliberately provoked the confrontation. Where an accused intentionally provokes another person into attacking them and then relies on that attack to justify force in self-defence, the defence fails — the accused cannot create their own self-defence situation through deliberate provocation. This is consistent with the People (AG) v Coffey principle that where self-defence is used as a pretext (i.e., the accused was actually the aggressor), the defence is unavailable. However, if the accused provoked the other person in a minor way and the response was disproportionate, there may be more nuanced analysis — the key is whether the accused deliberately engineered the confrontation. Option A incorrectly ignores the provocation issue. Option C incorrectly results in automatic manslaughter. Option D incorrectly separates provocation and self-defence doctrines in a way that is not the law.

questions.question: In DPP v Farrell [2014] (Ireland), the accused used force while attempting to escape from the scene of a crime and claimed self-defence when confronted by persons attempting to stop him. The court considered whether the accused was genuinely acting in self-defence. What was the finding?

options.A: The court held that self-defence was available — a person escaping from a crime scene has the same right to self-defence as any other person
options.B: The court found that where the force used was disproportionate to the threat faced during escape, and where the accused's own criminal conduct had placed them in the situation, the self-defence claim failed — the force used exceeded what was necessary in the circumstances and the accused was not genuinely acting defensively
options.C: The court held that self-defence is never available to a person fleeing from a crime scene — the accused's prior criminal conduct entirely bars the defence
options.D: The court held that the accused's criminal conduct was irrelevant to the self-defence assessment and that only the circumstances at the moment of using force were relevant

questions.explanation: DPP v Farrell [2014] illustrates how the circumstances surrounding the use of force are relevant to the self-defence assessment. The court found that where the accused was escaping from a criminal enterprise and used disproportionate force against persons attempting to prevent his escape, the self-defence claim failed. Multiple factors weighed against the defence: the disproportionality of the force used; the accused's own criminal conduct that created the situation; and the absence of a genuine defensive necessity. Farrell is an important practical illustration of how courts assess self-defence in factually complex situations — the theoretical availability of self-defence to anyone does not mean it will succeed in all circumstances. Option A is too broad — courts do not automatically extend full self-defence rights to persons in the middle of criminal conduct. Option C goes too far in making the defence categorically unavailable. Option D incorrectly isolates the moment of force from its broader context.

questions.question: Emma is walking home at night and is attacked by Frank. She genuinely believes Frank is about to kill her. In a panic, she uses a glass bottle to strike him, causing serious injury. In fact, Frank intended only to snatch her handbag and had no weapon. Is Emma's self-defence claim likely to succeed?

options.A: No — Emma's force was objectively disproportionate to the actual threat Frank posed; since Frank only intended a theft, Emma's use of a glass bottle was excessive
options.B: Yes — under s.18 NFOATP 1997, the test is based on the circumstances as Emma honestly believed them to be; if Emma genuinely believed she was about to be killed, the force used is assessed by reference to that belief; the fact that Frank's actual intention was less serious than Emma believed does not defeat the defence if her belief was honest; depending on the circumstances, her response may be within the range of what was reasonable given what she believed
options.C: No — self-defence requires that the actual threat be objectively proportionate to the force used; Emma must prove both what she believed and what was objectively true
options.D: Yes — but only if the court finds that a reasonable person would also have panicked in the same situation; the panic element requires proof that the reasonable person would have done the same

questions.explanation: This scenario tests the subjective basis of the s.18 NFOATP 1997 test for self-defence following Crawford v DPP [2024]. The critical feature of the Irish test is that force is assessed by reference to the circumstances as the accused honestly believed them to be — not as they objectively were. Emma genuinely believed she was about to be killed. The force she used (a glass bottle) must be assessed against her honest belief of imminent lethal danger, not against the actual (lesser) threat Frank posed. If her belief was genuine and the force was reasonable given what she believed, the defence may succeed even though her belief was mistaken. This is the classic 'putative self-defence' situation — the accused responds to a threat that they honestly but mistakenly believed to exist. The Dwyer doctrine would be relevant if her force was excessive even given her belief (in which case manslaughter rather than acquittal might result). Option A incorrectly applies the objective actual threat test. Option C incorrectly requires objective proportionality. Option D adds a panic element that is not part of the statutory test.
